subtopics.title: Definition and Scope of Accounting
common_misconceptions: Accounting is just adding numbers
Accounting and bookkeeping are the same
Only business owners need accounting information
subtopics.teaching_note: Accounting = process of identifying, measuring, recording, and communicating economic information to permit informed judgments. Bookkeeping = systematic recording of transactions (subset of accounting). Users: owners, managers, investors, creditors, government (FIRS), employees, public. Nigerian regulatory bodies: ICAN (est. 1965), ANAN, FRCN.

subtopics.title: Accounting Concepts and Conventions
common_misconceptions: Going concern means business will never close
Prudence means never making profit
All transactions can be recorded in accounting
subtopics.teaching_note: Key concepts: Business entity - business separate from owner. Going concern - assume business continues. Consistency - same method year to year. Prudence (conservatism) - don't anticipate profit, provide for all losses. Accrual - record when earned/incurred, not when cash moves. Matching - match revenues with expenses in same period. Money measurement - only record quantifiable monetary transactions.

subtopics.title: Principles of Double Entry
common_misconceptions: Debit always means increase (it increases assets, decreases liabilities)
Credit always means decrease (it decreases assets, increases liabilities/capital)
Accounting equation is always A = L + C (it's the basis)
subtopics.teaching_note: DEAD CLIC: Debit increases Expenses, Assets, Drawings. Credit increases Liabilities, Income, Capital. Accounting equation: Assets = Capital + Liabilities. Every debit has equal credit. Example: Started business with ₦100,000 cash. Dr Cash ₦100,000, Cr Capital ₦100,000. Bought goods for ₦20,000 cash. Dr Purchases ₦20,000, Cr Cash ₦20,000.

subtopics.title: Books of Original Entry
common_misconceptions: Cash book is part of the ledger (it's both a book of original entry AND part of ledger)
All transactions go through the journal
Source documents are not important in accounting
subtopics.teaching_note: Books of original entry: Sales Day Book (credit sales), Purchases Day Book (credit purchases), Returns Inwards (sales returns), Returns Outwards (purchases returns), Cash Book (all cash/bank transactions), Petty Cash Book (small expenses), General Journal (opening entries, corrections, non-regular transactions). Source documents: invoice, receipt, debit note, credit note, cheque, voucher.

subtopics.title: Single and Double Column Cash Book
common_misconceptions: Trade discount appears in cash book (NO - only cash discount)
Discount allowed and received are the same (allowed = expense, received = income)
Cash book only records cash transactions
subtopics.teaching_note: Cash Book is both a book of original entry and a ledger account. Discount allowed: given to customers for prompt payment (expense, debit side). Discount received: received from suppliers (income, credit side). Three-column cash book: cash column, bank column, discount column. Trade discount: deducted before recording (not in books). Cash discount: recorded in cash book. Contra entries: when cash is banked or withdrawn (C marked on both sides).

subtopics.title: Bank Reconciliation Statement
common_misconceptions: Unpresented cheques are added to bank statement balance (they are deducted from bank statement)
Bank charges increase the bank balance (they decrease)
A dishonoured cheque increases the cash book balance (it decreases it)
subtopics.teaching_note: Starting from cash book balance: Add uncredited deposits (lodgements not on statement), deduct unpresented cheques. OR starting from bank statement: Deduct uncredited deposits, add unpresented cheques. Adjust cash book FIRST for items not yet recorded (bank charges, SO, DD, direct credits, dishonoured cheques). Then reconcile adjusted balance with statement. Unpresented cheques: cheque issued but not yet cashed. Uncredited deposits: cash paid in but not yet credited by bank.

subtopics.title: Preparation and Uses of Trial Balance
common_misconceptions: A balanced trial balance means no errors
Trial balance includes all accounts in the ledger
If trial balance doesn't balance, the error must be in arithmetic
subtopics.teaching_note: Trial balance = list of all ledger balances at a point in time. Total debits MUST equal total credits. Types of accounts: Personal (debtors = debit, creditors = credit), Real (assets = debit), Nominal (expenses/losses = debit, incomes/gains = credit). Uses: check arithmetic accuracy, starting point for final accounts. Errors NOT detected: omission, commission, principle, original entry, compensating errors, complete reversal.
